policy_statement: Propose launching at least 5 neighbourhood kitchens in the city.
mechanism_description: The city council is proposing to launch neighbourhood kitchens to support community food provision, which can reduce food waste and the amount of organic waste going to landfill, lowering methane emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Food provisioning practices → ↓ food waste to landfill → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Cities support community food provision (e.g., neighbourhood kitchens, shared meals, food redistribution) that improves planning, sharing, and utilization of food, resulting in less edible food being discarded and less organics entering the municipal waste stream.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced organic material in landfills lowers anaerobic decomposition of food waste, which is a major source of methane.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane (and potentially some CO₂e from reduced upstream food production and waste collection/processing) leading to reduced overall greenhouse gas emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing methane emissions by preventing food waste from being landfilled (and thus avoiding anaerobic decomposition in landfill).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Community food programs reduce edible food waste and organic waste sent to landfill, avoiding landfill methane generation—this is a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these programs can also improve social outcomes and may marginally reduce collection energy or enhance resilience (food security), the largest climate-relevant effect tied to this mechanism is avoided CH₄ from landfilled organics (plus upstream avoided emissions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “community kitchen” increases total cooking energy use without reducing waste, benefits could shrink; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes food-waste reduction and methane avoidance, so mitigation remains dominant for this mechanism class.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this mechanism (community food programs explicitly aimed at reducing food waste/landfilled organics) shares the same primary mitigation pathway through avoided landfill methane.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy also meaningfully delivers resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., reduced upstream production) or social resilience outcomes requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may have small countervailing emissions (added cooking energy, refrigeration, transport for redistribution) or may shift organics to composting/AD rather than pure prevention; nevertheless, the dominant intended climate effect for this mechanism is methane avoidance from reduced landfilling of food waste.
key_indicators: "Encourage 'neighbourhood kitchens' throughout the city"; "City Council is proposing ... to launch at least 5 'neighbourhood kitchens'"
co_benefits: Food security/affordability; community cohesion; public health/nutrition; local employment/skills
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program or infrastructure-investment if the underlying measure specifies funding, grants, or facility capex/opex; the provided text only indicates a proposal/initiative and a numeric rollout, so classified as a planning/program strategy.
